stem: The nurse cares for a 72-year-old male client in the neurological ICU showing signs of worsening intracranial pressure. Based on the clinical record, prioritize the nursing actions by dragging them into the correct order, from first to last.

options.text: Ensure head of bed is elevated to 30 degrees.
Notify the neurosurgeon of the change in status.

tabs.title: Clinical Feed
tabs.content:
Admission Note - 03:00
S: Client is a 72-year-old male admitted from the Emergency Department after being found unresponsive by his adult child.
B: History of hypertension and atrial fibrillation, for which he takes warfarin. Client lives alone in a rural area; transportation for follow-up care is a noted barrier. CT scan in ED revealed a large intracerebral hemorrhage. An external ventricular drain (EVD) was placed.
A: On admission to NICU, GCS is 11 (E3V3M5). Pupils 3mm, equal and reactive. Mild left hemiparesis noted. BP 165/92 mmHg, HR 65/min, RR 14/min, SpO2 95% on 2L NC.
R: Admit to NICU for close neurological monitoring. Maintain HOB at 30 degrees. NPO status. Follow neurosurgery recommendations.

Progress Note - 07:00
S: Reporting an acute change in neurological status for Mr. Miller.
B: 72-year-old male admitted at 03:00 for a right-sided hemorrhagic stroke. Warfarin was held on admission. INR remains elevated.
A: At 07:00, client is less responsive to stimuli. GCS has declined from 11 to 9 (E2V2M5). Vital signs now show evidence of Cushing's triad: BP is 180/100 mmHg, HR is 55/min, and RR is 10/min. SpO2 is 92% on 2L NC. Right pupil is now 4mm and sluggishly reactive; left pupil remains 3mm and brisk. New left-sided facial droop observed. ICP is 24 mmHg. Mannitol 25g IV was administered at 06:45 for ICP >20 mmHg.
R: Will immediately administer PRN labetalol for SBP >160 mmHg. Will notify neurosurgeon of this acute deterioration. Continue hourly neuro checks and prepare for potential airway intervention.

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
Admission Assessment (03:15)
Neurological: GCS 11 (E3V3M5). Oriented to person only. Pupils are 3mm, equal, round, and reactive to light. Mild left hemiparesis with 3/5 strength in LUE/LLE. Right-sided strength 5/5.
Cardiovascular: S1, S2 audible. Irregularly irregular rhythm. Pulses 2+ throughout.
Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Breathing non-labored.

Focused Assessment (07:00)
Neurological: GCS 9 (E2V2M5). Opens eyes to pain, incomprehensible sounds, withdraws from pain. Right pupil 4mm and sluggishly reactive to light. Left pupil 3mm and briskly reactive. New left facial droop noted. Left upper and lower extremity strength is now 1/5. Right-sided strength remains 4/5.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content:
CT Head without contrast (Time: 02:45)
INDICATION: Altered mental status, suspected stroke.
FINDINGS: Large acute intraparenchymal hemorrhage is identified in the right temporal lobe measuring approximately 5 x 4 cm. There is significant surrounding vasogenic edema. This results in approximately 8 mm of right-to-left midline shift. There is mass effect upon the right lateral ventricle.
IMPRESSION: Acute large right temporal intraparenchymal hemorrhage with significant mass effect and midline shift, consistent with hemorrhagic stroke. Urgent neurosurgical consultation is recommended.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content:
Order Date/TimeActionFrequency/Status
Today @ 03:10Admit to Neurological ICUStanding
Today @ 03:10NPOStanding
Today @ 03:10Neurological assessmentq1h
Today @ 03:10Maintain Head of Bed at 30 degreesStanding
Today @ 03:10External Ventricular Drain (EVD): maintain drain at 10 cmH2O above tragusStanding
Today @ 03:10Titrate O2 to maintain SpO2 >92%Standing
Today @ 03:15Labetalol 10mg IVPRN q6h for SBP > 160 mmHg
Today @ 03:15Mannitol 25g IVPRN q6h for ICP > 20 mmHg

tabs.title: MAR Console
tabs.content:
MedicationDoseRouteFrequencyLast Administered
Labetalol10 mgIVq6h PRN SBP > 160 mmHgNot Administered
Mannitol25 gIVq6h PRN ICP > 20 mmHgToday @ 06:45
Pantoprazole40 mgIVDailyToday @ 04:00
Warfarin5 mgPODailyHOLD on admission

rationale.correct: The client is exhibiting Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, decreased respirations), a late sign of severely increased intracranial pressure (ICP) and impending brain herniation. The priority is to execute immediate interventions to lower ICP. The correct sequence begins with the fastest, non-invasive action (elevating HOB), followed by a critical medication intervention based on standing orders (labetalol), then communicating the crisis to the provider, performing a more detailed assessment, and finally, preparing for potential complications.
rationale.incorrect: Any other sequence delays critical, time-sensitive interventions. For example, notifying the provider before taking action on existing orders wastes precious minutes while ICP continues to rise. Performing a full neurological assessment before administering labetalol allows the dangerously high blood pressure to worsen cerebral edema. Prioritizing intubation preparation is anticipatory and should not precede immediate interventions that might prevent the need for it.

answerBreakdown.content: Correct Position 1. Elevating the head of the bed to 30 degrees is the fastest, non-pharmacological intervention to promote venous drainage from the brain, which can immediately help to lower ICP. It should be the first action.

Correct Position 2. Administering IV labetalol is the next priority. The client's severe hypertension (SBP 180) is acutely worsening the ICP. A standing PRN order allows the nurse to act immediately to control this critical factor.

Correct Position 3. After initiating immediate stabilizing actions, the neurosurgeon must be notified. This communication is critical for obtaining further orders, such as for hypertonic saline or consideration of emergency surgical decompression.

Correct Position 4. A focused neurological assessment is essential to establish a new baseline and evaluate the client's response to the interventions. However, it follows the immediate life-saving actions of positioning and medication administration.

Correct Position 5. Preparing for intubation is an essential anticipatory action. With a GCS of 9 and a declining respiratory rate, the client is at high risk for losing their protective airway reflexes. This preparation occurs once immediate ICP-lowering measures are underway.

clinicalPearls: Cushing's triad (hypertension with a widening pulse pressure, bradycardia, and irregular respirations) is a late, ominous sign of severely increased ICP indicating pressure on the brainstem.

Cerebral Perfusion Pressure (CPP) is calculated as Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) minus Intracranial Pressure (ICP). The goal is typically 60-70 mmHg. Uncontrolled hypertension can worsen vasogenic edema and must be managed carefully.
In clients with increased ICP, the head should be kept in a neutral, midline position to optimize venous outflow from the brain and prevent jugular vein compression.
Recognizing social determinants of health, such as a client living in a rural area with transportation barriers, is crucial for effective discharge planning even during an acute crisis.
rationale.questionTrap: A common trap is prioritizing communication (notifying the provider) over direct, immediate actions the nurse can take based on existing orders. In a neurological emergency, the nurse must first initiate available life-saving interventions like positioning and administering PRN medications before making the call.
rationale.mnemonic:
For ICP crisis priorities, think 'ICP-NOW!':
I: Increase HOB
C: Control BP
P: Phone the provider
N: Neuro assessment
O: Oxygen & Airway prep
W: Watch for changes

pedagogy.nclexCategory: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential
